mnemonics.title: DAC vs MAC
mnemonics.content: D-A-C: Data Owner Chooses | M-A-C: Mandatory Admin Classification

mnemonics.title: RBAC Steps
mnemonics.content: U-P-R: Users get assigned to Permissions through Roles

mnemonics.title: SQL Privileges
mnemonics.content: S-I-U-D-E: Select Insert Update Delete Execute - GRABBED to users

mnemonics.title: ABAC Factors
mnemonics.content: U-R-E: User attributes + Resource attributes + Environment conditions

acronyms.usage: Model where data owners control who accesses their data using GRANT/REVOKE statements
System-enforced model using security labels/classification levels for access decisions
Access control based on job roles; users assigned to roles get associated permissions
Policy-based access using user, resource, and environment attributes for fine-grained control
Standard language for managing and accessing relational databases
Protocols for encrypting data in transit between client and database

visualTips.visualization: Think of a ladder: DAC = bottom (user has control), MAC = top (government/military strict), RBAC = middle steps (job-based roles)
Draw three circles: DAC (user decides), MAC (system decides), RBAC (role decides). Show how permissions flow differently in each.
GRANT = giving a key to open a door, REVOKE = taking back that key. Arrow pointing TO user for GRANT, AWAY from user for REVOKE.
Picture a filter/screen that separates SQL code from user input, showing how parameterized queries block malicious code.

analogies.analogy: Like a homeowner deciding who can enter their house - they have the keys and decide whom to give copies to.
Like a top-secret government facility where security clearance levels strictly determine access - no one can override the system.
Like a company org chart - employees get access based on their job title (role), not individual preferences.
Like a VIP club checking multiple criteria (ID, membership type, tonight's event, guest list) before allowing entry.
Like wearing sunglasses - you see a filtered version of reality, hiding certain details from plain view.
Like giving a hotel room key that only opens your room, not the entire floor or master keys.
